title: Using agreement on direction of change to build rank-based emotion classifiers

reviewer_output.candidate_title: Using agreement on direction of change to build rank-based emotion classifiers

source_record_provenance.record_title: Using agreement on direction of change to build rank-based emotion classifiers

answers.answer_rationale: The full text describes MuLan as a cross-modal audio-text embedding model learned via a contrastive loss on (audio, text) pairs, with no mention of a preference-learning component (e.g., pairwise ranking, converted ratings, or RL loops). Supporting content focuses on cross-modal embedding, retrieval, and tagging rather than explicit preference learning.
supporting_quotes: MuLan takes the form of a two-tower, joint audio-text embedding model trained using 44 million music recordings (370K hours) and weakly-associated, free-form text annotations.

We demonstrate the versatility of the MuLan embeddings with a range of experiments including transfer learning, zero-shot music tagging, language understanding in the music domain, and cross-modal retrieval applications.
MuLan employs a two-tower parallel encoder architecture, using a contrastive loss objective that elicits a shared embedding space between music audio and text.
Our goal is to construct a shared embedding space for music audio and free-form natural language text, in which proximity is predictive of shared semantics both within and across modalities.

The LiPO framework describes LM alignment as a Learning-to-Rank (LTR) problem.

While the paper is applied within the audio domain and is not a survey, the absence of any preference learning evidence and the explicit exclusion criteria for preference-only evaluation papers lead to a clear exclusion recommendation.

round-B_SeniorLead_output.candidate_title: Gemini 1.5: Unlocking multimodal understanding across millions of tokens of context

source_record_provenance.record_title: Gemini 1.5: Unlocking multimodal understanding across millions of tokens of context

round-B_SeniorLead_output.decision_rationale: The full text and synthesis provide direct counterevidence showing absence of any preference learning signals such as ranking, comparative preference signals, or reinforcement learning loops involving preferences in the audio domain or multimodal context. Although the paper pertains to audio and multimodal settings and is not a survey, its focus is on model capabilities and evaluations without preference-based learning components. This satisfies exclusion criteria that exclude studies using preferences solely for evaluation or lacking learning components. Therefore, the paper is excluded based on clear direct negative evidence.
